findings.title: Insecure Deserialization via pickle.loads() with User Input
findings.description:
Two test cases deserialize user-controlled input using pickle.loads(), which can execute
arbitrary code during deserialization.

1. BenchmarkTest00164.py (line 47): Direct form input to pickle.
   SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00164")
   GUARD: String concatenation/slicing (extracts param exactly)
   SINK: pickle.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(bar))

2. BenchmarkTest00078.py (line 53): Cookie input to pickle.
   SOURCE: request.cookies.get("BenchmarkTest00078") -> unquote_plus
   GUARD: String concatenation/slicing (extracts param exactly)
   SINK: pickle.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(bar))
findings.impact:
Python pickle deserialization can execute arbitrary code via __reduce__ methods in crafted
payloads. An attacker can craft a base64-encoded pickle payload that executes system commands,
establishes reverse shells, or exfiltrates data.
findings.remediation:
Never use pickle to deserialize untrusted data. Use safe serialization formats like JSON.
If pickle is required, implement a RestrictedUnpickler that only allows specific safe classes.

findings.title: Code Injection via exec() and eval() with User Input
findings.description:
Multiple test cases pass user-controlled input to exec() or eval(), enabling arbitrary
code execution. Affected files:

1. BenchmarkTest00160.py (line 49): Direct exec() with user input.
   SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00160")
   GUARD: match/case always takes case A, bar = param
   SINK: exec(bar)

2. BenchmarkTest00159.py (line 45): exec() with user input via list manipulation.
   SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00159")
   GUARD: list append/pop leaves param at index 0
   SINK: exec(bar)

3. BenchmarkTest00074.py (line 51): exec() with cookie input via configparser.
   SOURCE: request.cookies -> urllib.parse.unquote_plus
   GUARD: configparser passthrough (no sanitization)
   SINK: exec(bar)

4. BenchmarkTest00073.py (line 54): eval() with cookie input.
   SOURCE: request.cookies -> urllib.parse.unquote_plus
   GUARD: String slicing plus quote validation (bypassable)
   SINK: eval(bar)
findings.impact:
An attacker can execute arbitrary Python code on the server, leading to complete system
compromise including data exfiltration, lateral movement, and denial of service.
findings.remediation:
Never use exec() or eval() with user-controlled input. Use ast.literal_eval() for safe
literal parsing if needed. Redesign the logic to avoid dynamic code execution.

findings.title: OS Command Injection via subprocess.run with shell=True
findings.description:
Multiple test cases pass user-controlled input into shell commands via subprocess.run
with shell=True. Affected files:

1. BenchmarkTest00167.py (lines 50-53): User input flows to shell command.
   SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00167")
   GUARD: Conditional always routes to param (should is in the test string)
   SINK: subprocess.run("sh -c echo {bar}", shell=True)

2. BenchmarkTest00166.py (lines 39, 50-53): User input flows via dict passthrough.
   SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00166")
   GUARD: dict set/get (no sanitization)
   SINK: subprocess.run("sh -c echo {bar}", shell=True)

3. BenchmarkTest00165.py (lines 42, 55-57): User input flows via list manipulation.
   SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00165")
   GUARD: list append/pop leaves param at index 0
   SINK: subprocess.run(["sh", "-c", "echo {bar}"])

False positives: BenchmarkTest00512.py and BenchmarkTest00350.py use safe constant values.
findings.impact:
An attacker can execute arbitrary OS commands on the server by injecting shell metacharacters
into the form parameter.
findings.remediation:
Never use shell=True with user-controlled input. Use subprocess.run with a list of arguments
and avoid string interpolation into shell commands. Apply strict input validation.

findings.title: SQL Injection via String Interpolation in Multiple Endpoints
findings.description:
Multiple test cases construct SQL queries using f-string interpolation with user input,
enabling SQL injection. Affected files:

1. BenchmarkTest00099.py (line 46): request.form -> configparser passthrough -> f-string SQL
   SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00099")
   GUARD: configparser set/get (no sanitization)
   SINK: cur.execute(f"SELECT username from USERS where password = '{bar}'")

2. BenchmarkTest00283.py (line 46): request.form via request_wrapper -> dict passthrough
   SOURCE: wrapped.get_form_parameter("BenchmarkTest00283")
   GUARD: dict set/get (no sanitization)
   SINK: cur.execute(f"SELECT username from USERS where password = '{bar}'")

3. BenchmarkTest00284.py (line 44): request.form -> conditional always assigns param
   SOURCE: wrapped.get_form_parameter("BenchmarkTest00284")
   GUARD: 'should' in bar always True, so param assigned to bar
   SINK: cur.execute(f"SELECT username from USERS where password = '{bar}'")

BenchmarkTest00011.py and BenchmarkTest00012.py use parameterized queries and are safe.
findings.impact:
An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via the form parameter, allowing them to extract
all database contents, modify data, or potentially achieve code execution depending on
SQLite extensions.
findings.remediation: Use parameterized queries: cur.execute("SELECT username from USERS where password = ?", (bar,))

findings.title: Insecure YAML deserialization via yaml.Loader in BenchmarkTest00269
findings.description:
User input from a form parameter flows through ThingFactory.doSomething()
(which returns the input unchanged when using Thing1) and is then passed
to yaml.load() with the unsafe yaml.Loader. The yaml.Loader class supports
arbitrary Python object instantiation via YAML tags (e.g., !!python/object/apply).

SOURCE: request.form.getlist("BenchmarkTest00269")[0] (line 31-34)
GUARD: ThingFactory.doSomething() - identity function, does not sanitize
SINK: yaml.load(bar, Loader=yaml.Loader) (line 44)
findings.impact:
An attacker can achieve arbitrary code execution by submitting a YAML payload
containing Python object construction tags. For example:
!!python/object/apply:os.system ['id'] would execute the 'id' command on the
server. yaml.Loader permits deserialization of arbitrary Python objects.
findings.remediation:
Replace yaml.Loader with yaml.SafeLoader or use yaml.safe_load() instead:
  yobj = yaml.safe_load(bar)

findings.title: Insecure deserialization via pickle.loads in BenchmarkTest00164
findings.description:
User input from a form parameter is base64-decoded and passed directly to
pickle.loads(). The string concatenation/slicing transformation (prepend "help",
append "snapes on a plane", slice [4:-17]) exactly recovers the original
user-supplied value.

SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00164") (line 31)
GUARD: None - string operations preserve original tainted data
SINK: pickle.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(bar)) (line 47)
findings.impact:
An attacker can achieve arbitrary code execution on the server by submitting a
malicious pickle payload (base64-encoded) as the form parameter. This is the
most severe class of vulnerability as it grants full control of the server.
findings.remediation:
Never use pickle.loads() on untrusted input. Use JSON or another safe
serialization format. If pickle is required, use a RestrictedUnpickler
with a strict whitelist of allowed classes.

findings.title: Insecure deserialization via pickle.loads in BenchmarkTest00078
findings.description:
A cookie value controlled by the user is base64-decoded and passed directly to
pickle.loads(). The cookie is URL-decoded, then string slicing extracts the
original user-supplied portion, which is then base64-decoded and deserialized.

SOURCE: request.cookies.get("BenchmarkTest00078") (line 39)
GUARD: None - string concatenation/slicing does not sanitize; it merely
       prepends "help" and appends "snapes on a plane", then slices [4:-17]
       which exactly recovers the original param value
SINK: pickle.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(bar)) (line 53)
findings.impact:
An attacker can achieve arbitrary code execution on the server by crafting a
malicious pickle payload, base64-encoding it, and setting it as the cookie value.
pickle.loads() can instantiate arbitrary Python objects and execute code during
deserialization.
findings.remediation:
Never use pickle.loads() on untrusted input. Use a safe serialization format
such as JSON. If pickle must be used, implement a RestrictedUnpickler that
only allows a whitelist of safe classes.

findings.title: Hardcoded Flask secret_key enables session forgery
findings.description:
The Flask application uses a hardcoded secret key:
  app.secret_key = b'if you need this you are doing something wrong'

SOURCE: Hardcoded bytes literal in source code (line 25)
GUARD: None - the key is static and publicly visible in the repository
SINK: Flask session signing mechanism (used by Flask internally for cookie signing)

While the comment suggests awareness of the issue, the key is still set and active.
Any attacker with access to the source code (or who guesses the key) can forge
session cookies and tamper with signed data.
findings.impact:
An attacker can craft arbitrary Flask session cookies, potentially impersonating
other users, bypassing any session-based access controls, or injecting malicious
session data. Since this is a benchmark application, the risk is contextual, but
the pattern is a clear anti-pattern that should not be replicated.
findings.remediation:
Generate the secret key from a cryptographically secure source and load it from
an environment variable or secure configuration file:

  import os
  app.secret_key = os.environ.get('FLASK_SECRET_KEY', os.urandom(32))

findings.title: Path traversal in app.py show_page via unvalidated path parameter
findings.description:
The show_page route handler accepts a user-controlled path parameter (mypath) and passes it
directly to render_template() and send_from_directory() without validating that the path
does not contain directory traversal sequences (e.g., "../").

SOURCE: Flask route parameter <path:mypath> (line 37)
GUARD: Only checks if path ends with ".html" or "404.html" - no traversal validation
SINK: render_template(f"web/{mypath}") (line 39) and send_from_directory(..., mypath) (line 41)

While send_from_directory has some built-in protections, render_template with an
f-string path does not prevent traversal. An attacker could request
/benchmark/../../secret.html to attempt to render templates outside the intended directory.
findings.impact:
An attacker could read arbitrary template files from the filesystem by crafting paths with
directory traversal sequences. This could expose configuration files, source code, or
other sensitive data accessible to the application process.
findings.remediation:
Validate the mypath parameter to ensure it does not contain ".." sequences or absolute
path components. Use werkzeug.utils.safe_join() to construct the path safely, or apply
an allowlist of valid path prefixes.

findings.title: No Authentication or Authorization on Any Endpoint
findings.description:
The Flask application registers approximately 70+ routes across app.py and all testcode/
modules without any authentication or authorization checks. Every endpoint is publicly
accessible including those that execute SQL queries, OS commands, Python code,
deserialize untrusted data, and query LDAP directories.
No middleware, decorators, or session checks are applied to any route.
findings.impact:
Any user on the network can access all application functionality without authentication.
Combined with the injection vulnerabilities, an anonymous attacker can achieve arbitrary
code execution, data exfiltration, and full system compromise.
findings.remediation:
Implement authentication middleware (e.g., Flask-Login) that requires valid credentials
before accessing any application endpoint. Apply role-based authorization to restrict
access to sensitive functionality.

findings.title: LDAP Injection via Unsanitized User Input in Filter Construction
findings.description:
Multiple test cases construct LDAP search filters using f-string interpolation with
user-controlled input, enabling LDAP injection attacks.

1. BenchmarkTest00265.py (line 42): Direct form input to LDAP filter.
   SOURCE: request.form.getlist("BenchmarkTest00265")[0]
   GUARD: None (bar = param directly)
   SINK: conn.search(base, "(&(objectclass=person)(|(uid={bar})(street=...)))")

2. BenchmarkTest00266.py (line 53): Form input via match/case routes to param.
   SOURCE: request.form.getlist("BenchmarkTest00266")[0]
   GUARD: match/case (guess=A, so bar=param)
   SINK: conn.search(base, "(&(objectclass=person)(|(uid={bar})(street=...)))")

3. BenchmarkTest00427.py (line 47): Form key name used as LDAP filter input.
   SOURCE: request.form key matching
   GUARD: dict passthrough (no sanitization)
   SINK: conn.search(base, "(&(objectclass=person)(uid={bar}))")

False positives: BenchmarkTest00077.py (bar=bob), BenchmarkTest00076.py (bar=keyA value).
findings.impact:
An attacker can manipulate LDAP queries to bypass authentication, extract unauthorized
data, or modify directory entries by injecting LDAP metacharacters.
findings.remediation: Escape special LDAP characters using ldap3.utils.conv.escape_filter_chars() or equivalent.

findings.title: Plaintext Passwords Stored in SQLite Database Initialization
findings.description:
User credentials are stored in plaintext in the database via hardcoded INSERT statements:
User01/P455w0rd, User02/B3nchM3rk, User03/a$c11, foo/bar.
SOURCE: Hardcoded plaintext passwords in SQL statements.
GUARD: None - no hashing or encryption applied.
SINK: SQLite database USERS table password column.
findings.impact:
Database compromise exposes all user passwords in cleartext. Attackers can use these
credentials for credential stuffing attacks against other systems. The passwords are also
visible in the source code.
findings.remediation:
Hash passwords using bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 before storage. Never store plaintext
passwords. Remove hardcoded credentials from source code.

findings.title: Hardcoded LDAP Admin Credentials in helpers/ldap.py
findings.description:
LDAP connections use hardcoded admin credentials in two locations:
Line 5 and Line 19 both use password='secret' with user='uid=admin,ou=system'.
Additionally, test user passwords are hardcoded on line 6 (MrFooPa$$word, barM$B4dPass, YouwontGue$$).
SOURCE: Hardcoded string literals.
GUARD: None.
SINK: ldap3.Connection() authentication parameters and add_entry() userpassword attributes.
findings.impact:
Anyone with access to the source code can obtain the LDAP admin credentials and all user passwords.
An attacker could use these credentials to authenticate to the LDAP server, read/modify all entries,
and potentially escalate privileges across connected systems.
findings.remediation:
Store LDAP credentials in environment variables or a secrets manager. Use a dedicated
service account with least-privilege access rather than an admin account.
Hash user passwords before storing in LDAP.

findings.title: Hardcoded Flask Secret Key in app.py
findings.description:
The Flask application uses a hardcoded secret key: app.secret_key = b'if you need this you are doing something wrong'.
This key is used by Flask to sign session cookies and other security-sensitive tokens.
SOURCE: Hardcoded byte string in source code.
GUARD: None.
SINK: Flask session management (app.secret_key).
findings.impact:
An attacker who knows the secret key can forge Flask session cookies, potentially impersonating
any user. Since the key is committed to source code, anyone with repository access can extract it.
This enables session hijacking and privilege escalation attacks.
findings.remediation:
Load the secret key from an environment variable or a secrets manager.
Ensure the key is generated with a cryptographically secure random generator and is at least 32 bytes.

findings.title: Path traversal via ThingFactory pass-through in BenchmarkTest00003
findings.description:
A cookie value controlled by the user is URL-decoded and passed through
ThingFactory.doSomething(), which (for Thing1) returns the input unchanged.
The tainted value is then used as a filename component in a codecs.open() call
without any path validation.

SOURCE: request.cookies.get("BenchmarkTest00003") (line 39)
GUARD: ThingFactory.doSomething() - identity function, does not sanitize
SINK: codecs.open(f'{helpers.utils.TESTFILES_DIR}/{bar}', 'r', 'utf-8') (line 50)
findings.impact:
An attacker can read arbitrary files on the server by setting the cookie to
a path traversal payload such as "../../../../etc/passwd".
findings.remediation:
Validate that the resolved path stays within TESTFILES_DIR:
  real = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(TESTFILES_DIR, bar))
  if not real.startswith(os.path.realpath(TESTFILES_DIR)):
      abort(403)

findings.title: Path traversal via unsanitized cookie in BenchmarkTest00002
findings.description:
A cookie value controlled by the user is URL-decoded and assigned to bar via
a conditional that always evaluates to the tainted path. The string "This
should never happen" contains 'should', so the condition on line 42 is True,
and bar = param. The tainted value is then used in a file open call.

SOURCE: request.cookies.get("BenchmarkTest00002") (line 39)
GUARD: None - conditional on line 42-43 always assigns bar = param
SINK: codecs.open(f'{helpers.utils.TESTFILES_DIR}/{bar}', 'r', 'utf-8') (line 49)
findings.impact:
An attacker can read arbitrary files on the server by setting the cookie to
a path traversal payload such as "../../../../etc/passwd".
findings.remediation:
Validate that the resolved path stays within TESTFILES_DIR:
  real = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(TESTFILES_DIR, bar))
  if not real.startswith(os.path.realpath(TESTFILES_DIR)):
      abort(403)

findings.title: Path traversal via unsanitized cookie in BenchmarkTest00001
findings.description:
A cookie value controlled by the user is URL-decoded and used directly as a
filename component in a codecs.open() call. No path validation or sanitization
is performed. The file path is constructed by concatenating the TESTFILES_DIR
constant with the user-supplied value.

SOURCE: request.cookies.get("BenchmarkTest00001") (line 39)
GUARD: None - direct assignment bar = param
SINK: codecs.open(f'{helpers.utils.TESTFILES_DIR}/{bar}', 'r', 'utf-8') (line 47)
findings.impact:
An attacker can read arbitrary files on the server by setting the cookie to a
path traversal payload such as "../../../../etc/passwd". This can expose
sensitive configuration files, credentials, source code, and other data.
findings.remediation:
Validate that the resolved path stays within TESTFILES_DIR using
os.path.realpath() and os.path.commonpath(), or use a whitelist of
allowed filenames.

findings.title: XXE via external entity processing in BenchmarkTest00460
findings.description:
User input from a custom HTTP header (BenchmarkTest00460) is assigned directly
to bar and then parsed as XML with external entity processing explicitly
enabled via feature_external_ges=True.

SOURCE: request.headers.get("BenchmarkTest00460") (line 31)
GUARD: None - direct assignment bar = param
SINK: xml.dom.minidom.parseString(bar, parser) with feature_external_ges=True (line 45)
findings.impact:
An attacker can read arbitrary files from the server, perform SSRF, or cause
denial of service by including XML with external entity declarations in the
request header.
findings.remediation:
Remove the line enabling external entity processing:
  parser.setFeature(xml.sax.handler.feature_external_ges, True)

findings.title: XXE via external entity processing in BenchmarkTest00294
findings.description:
User input from a form parameter is assigned to bar via a conditional check.
The condition tests whether 'should' is NOT in "This should never happen" -
since 'should' IS in that string, the else branch executes and bar = param.
The tainted XML is then parsed with feature_external_ges explicitly enabled.

SOURCE: wrapped.get_form_parameter("BenchmarkTest00294") (line 34)
GUARD: None - the conditional on lines 39-42 always takes the else branch,
       passing user input through unchanged
SINK: xml.dom.minidom.parseString(bar, parser) with feature_external_ges=True (line 52)
findings.impact:
An attacker can read arbitrary files from the server, perform SSRF, or cause
denial of service by submitting XML with external entity declarations.
findings.remediation:
Remove the line enabling external entity processing:
  parser.setFeature(xml.sax.handler.feature_external_ges, True)

findings.title: XXE via external entity processing in BenchmarkTest00205
findings.description:
User input from a form parameter flows through a configparser round-trip
(which preserves the value) and is then parsed as XML with external entity
processing explicitly enabled. The SAX parser has feature_external_ges set
to True on line 51, which enables resolution of external general entities.

SOURCE: request.form.getlist("BenchmarkTest00205")[0] (line 31-34)
GUARD: None - configparser round-trip preserves tainted data
SINK: xml.dom.minidom.parseString(bar, parser) with feature_external_ges=True (line 53)
findings.impact:
An attacker can read arbitrary files from the server filesystem, perform SSRF
attacks against internal services, or cause denial of service via entity
expansion (billion laughs attack).
findings.remediation:
Remove the line enabling external entity processing:
  parser.setFeature(xml.sax.handler.feature_external_ges, True)
By default, external entities are disabled in Python's SAX parser.

findings.title: Reflected XSS via configparser round-trip in BenchmarkTest00098
findings.description:
User input from request.form["BenchmarkTest00098"] is stored into a
configparser section and then immediately retrieved. The configparser
round-trip preserves the tainted value. The value is then placed into the
HTTP response via str.format() without any HTML encoding.

SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00098") (line 31)
GUARD: None - configparser set/get round-trip does not sanitize
SINK: RESPONSE += 'bar is \'{0[bar]}\'...'.format(dict) returned as HTTP response (line 49)
findings.impact:
An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into the response by submitting
a crafted value in the form parameter. This enables session hijacking, credential
theft, and other client-side attacks.
findings.remediation:
Apply escape_for_html() to bar before including it in the response:
  dict['bar'] = escape_for_html(bar)

findings.title: Reflected XSS via unsanitized form input in BenchmarkTest00097
findings.description:
User input from request.form["BenchmarkTest00097"] is split on spaces and the
first token is assigned to bar. This value is then placed directly into the
HTTP response without HTML encoding. Splitting on spaces does not prevent XSS
since payloads like <script>alert(1)</script> contain no spaces.

SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00097") (line 31)
GUARD: param.split(' ')[0] - splits on space but does not sanitize HTML characters
SINK: RESPONSE += f'bar is \'{bar}\' ...' returned as HTTP response (line 42-43)
findings.impact:
An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the form parameter. Since
the only transformation is splitting on spaces, any XSS payload without spaces
(e.g., <img/src=x/onerror=alert(1)>) passes through unmodified.
findings.remediation:
Apply escape_for_html() to bar before including it in the response:
  RESPONSE += f'bar is \'{escape_for_html(bar)}\' and otherarg is \'{otherarg}\''

findings.title: Reflected XSS via unsanitized form input in BenchmarkTest00096
findings.description:
User input from request.form["BenchmarkTest00096"] flows into the HTTP response
without any HTML encoding or sanitization. The match/case statement on line 39
always takes the 'A' branch (since possible="ABC" and guess=possible[0]='A'),
assigning bar=param directly. The tainted value is then interpolated into
the response via f-string on line 51.

SOURCE: request.form.get("BenchmarkTest00096") (line 31)
GUARD: None - escape_for_html is imported but never called
SINK: RESPONSE += f'bar is \'{bar}\' ...' returned as HTTP response (line 51-54)
findings.impact:
An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into the response by submitting
a crafted value in the BenchmarkTest00096 form parameter. This enables session
hijacking, credential theft, defacement, and phishing attacks against other users.
findings.remediation:
Apply escape_for_html() to the bar variable before including it in the response:
  RESPONSE += f'bar is \'{escape_for_html(bar)}\' and otherarg is \'{otherarg}\''

findings.title: ThingFactory uses getattr without validation, enabling arbitrary class instantiation
findings.description:
The createThing() function reads a class name from a configuration file and uses
getattr() to dynamically resolve it against the ThingFactory module. There is no
validation that the resolved name is an allowed class.

SOURCE: config.get('DEFAULT', 'thing', fallback='Thing1') from ThingFactory.ini (line 7)
GUARD: None - any class/function name in the module can be resolved
SINK: getattr(helpers.ThingFactory, cname) followed by cobj() instantiation (lines 8-9)

The code:
  cname = config.get('DEFAULT', 'thing', fallback='Thing1')
  cobj = getattr(helpers.ThingFactory, cname)
  obj = cobj()

If the configuration file is tampered with, any callable in the ThingFactory module
(or imported into it) could be invoked. Since configparser is used, and other test
cases write to configparser sections with user input, configuration injection is
a realistic attack vector.
findings.impact:
An attacker who can modify ThingFactory.ini could cause arbitrary class
instantiation from the ThingFactory module namespace. This could be escalated
if additional imports are added to the module in the future.

findings.title: LDAP hardcoded admin credentials in helpers/ldap.py
findings.description:
The LDAP helper module contains hardcoded administrator credentials used for
both the initial connection setup and the get_connection() function:

  conn = ldap3.Connection(server, user='uid=admin,ou=system', password='secret', ...)
  ...
  def get_connection():
      conn = ldap3.Connection(server, user='uid=admin,ou=system', password='secret', ...)

SOURCE: Hardcoded string literals 'uid=admin,ou=system' and 'secret' (lines 5, 19)
GUARD: None - credentials are static and visible in source code
SINK: LDAP connection authentication (lines 5, 19)

While this uses MOCK_SYNC strategy for testing purposes, the pattern of hardcoding
credentials in source code establishes a dangerous precedent.
findings.impact:
Hardcoded credentials in source code are visible to anyone with repository access.
If this pattern is copied to a production context, it would allow unauthorized
LDAP access. The admin-level binding also means all LDAP queries run with full
privileges rather than least-privilege access.
findings.remediation:
Load LDAP credentials from environment variables or a secure configuration store:

  import os
  LDAP_USER = os.environ.get('LDAP_BIND_USER', 'uid=admin,ou=system')
  LDAP_PASS = os.environ.get('LDAP_BIND_PASS')
  if not LDAP_PASS:
      raise EnvironmentError("LDAP_BIND_PASS must be set")

findings.title: Dynamic module loading from filesystem without validation in app.py
findings.description:
The application dynamically imports all Python files from the testcode/ directory
whose filenames start with "Benchmark" using importlib.import_module(). There is
no validation that the files are legitimate test modules, no integrity check, and
no allowlist of expected modules.

SOURCE: os.listdir('testcode') - filesystem directory listing (line 27)
GUARD: Only checks filename starts with "Benchmark" and ends with ".py" (line 30)
SINK: importlib.import_module() and testmod.init(app) (lines 31-32)

If an attacker can write a file named BenchmarkTestEvil.py to the testcode/
directory, it will be automatically loaded and its init() function called with
the Flask app object.
findings.impact:
An attacker who gains write access to the testcode/ directory can achieve
arbitrary code execution by placing a malicious Python file that matches the
naming convention. The malicious code would execute with the full privileges
of the Flask application.
findings.remediation:
Use an explicit allowlist of expected test module names, or verify file
integrity (e.g., checksums) before importing. Consider importing from a
configuration manifest rather than scanning the filesystem:

  ALLOWED_MODULES = ['BenchmarkTest00001', 'BenchmarkTest00002', ...]
  for name in ALLOWED_MODULES:
      testmod = importlib.import_module(f'testcode.{name}')
      testmod.init(app)

findings.title: No CSRF protection on POST endpoints across all test case routes
findings.description:
The Flask application does not configure any CSRF protection mechanism. There is no
Flask-WTF CSRFProtect extension, no custom CSRF token validation, and no
SameSite cookie attribute on the session cookie. All ~70 POST endpoints accept
form submissions without any origin or token verification.

SOURCE: Any cross-origin form submission targeting /benchmark/*/BenchmarkTest* POST routes
GUARD: None - no CSRF tokens, no Referer/Origin header validation, no SameSite cookies
SINK: All POST handlers process form data and execute sensitive operations (SQL queries,
      file access, command execution, deserialization, LDAP queries, redirects)

While this is a benchmark application, the complete absence of CSRF protection
means any malicious website can trigger actions on behalf of an authenticated user
who has the benchmark application open in their browser.
findings.impact:
An attacker can craft a malicious webpage that submits forms to any of the benchmark
POST endpoints, triggering SQL queries, command injection, file access, or other
sensitive operations using the victim's browser session and cookies.
findings.remediation:
Enable Flask-WTF CSRF protection:

  from flask_wtf.csrf import CSRFProtect
  csrf = CSRFProtect(app)

Alternatively, set SameSite=Strict on cookies and validate the Origin header
on all state-changing requests.

findings.title: Database connection opened at module import time without error handling
findings.description:
The db_sqlite.py module executes database initialization (connect, DROP TABLE,
CREATE TABLE, INSERT) at module import time with no try/except guard. Since app.py
dynamically imports test modules, and those modules import helpers.db_sqlite, any
failure in database initialization will crash the entire application startup.

SOURCE: sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH) at module scope (line 6)
GUARD: None - no exception handling around the connection or executescript
SINK: Application fails to start if DB_PATH is inaccessible or disk is full

The module also does not close the cursor explicitly (only the connection),
and sets con.autocommit = True at the module level which affects the initial
connection only.
findings.impact:
If the testfiles directory is missing, read-only, or the disk is full, the
application will fail to start with an unhandled sqlite3.OperationalError.
The DROP TABLE IF EXISTS / CREATE TABLE pattern also means every application
restart destroys all existing data without warning.

findings.title: Global mutable state in helpers/utils.py shared across all requests
findings.description:
The helpers/utils.py module defines global mutable state that is shared across all
Flask requests without any synchronization:

  mysession = {}          (line 6)
  sharedstr = 'default shared string'  (line 8)

SOURCE: Module-level mutable dict and string (lines 6, 8)
GUARD: None - no locking, no per-request isolation
SINK: Multiple test cases read/write mysession (BenchmarkTest00025-00033) and
      sharedstr (BenchmarkTest00078, BenchmarkTest00164)

In a multi-threaded or multi-worker Flask deployment, concurrent requests can
race on these shared variables. The mysession dict is used as a pseudo-session
store for "remember me" cookies, and sharedstr is used as a cross-request
communication channel in deserialization tests.
findings.impact:
Race conditions on mysession could cause session data corruption, allowing one
user's session to leak into another user's response. The sharedstr variable
allows one request's deserialization side-effect to be observed by another,
which is an intentional benchmark behavior but represents a trust boundary
violation in any real application.
findings.remediation:
Replace the global mysession dict with Flask's built-in session mechanism or
a proper server-side session store (e.g., flask-session with Redis). If global
state is truly needed, use threading.Lock for synchronization:

  import threading
  _session_lock = threading.Lock()
  mysession = {}

  def set_session(key, value):
      with _session_lock:
          mysession[key] = value

findings.title: SQL query result reflected in HTML response without escaping in db_sqlite.results()
findings.description:
The db_sqlite.results() function renders SQL query results directly into an HTML
response body without applying HTML escaping. Both the SQL query string and the
row data from the database are interpolated into HTML without sanitization.

SOURCE: Database row values from cur iteration (line 47), sql parameter (line 43)
GUARD: None - no escaping applied to either the query string or the row data
SINK: HTML response string returned to the browser (lines 43, 47)

The function code:
  if not cur:
      ret += f'Results set is empty for query: {sql}'  # sql unescaped
  ...
  for row in cur:
      ret += ', '.join(str(x) for x in row)  # row data unescaped

If stored XSS payloads exist in the database, or if the SQL string itself contains
user-controlled input, the raw values will be rendered as HTML.
findings.impact:
An attacker who can influence data stored in the database (via SQL injection in another
endpoint, or through legitimate data entry) could inject JavaScript that executes when
another user views query results. The unescaped SQL string in error messages could also
reflect user input as HTML.
findings.remediation:
Apply escape_for_html() from helpers.utils to both the SQL string and each row value
before embedding in the HTML response:

  from helpers.utils import escape_for_html
  ...
  ret += f'Results set is empty for query: {escape_for_html(sql)}'
  ...
  ret += ', '.join(escape_for_html(str(x)) for x in row)

findings.title: get_parameter returns None without null guard, callers do not check
findings.description:
The get_parameter() helper function returns None when neither request.args nor
request.form contain the requested parameter. The function has no default value
fallback and does not raise an exception for missing parameters.

SOURCE: request.args.get(param) and request.form.get(param) (lines 78, 80)
GUARD: None - the function returns None if both lookups miss
SINK: Callers that use the return value in string operations, SQL queries, etc.

The function code:
  def get_parameter(request, param):
      ret = request.args.get(param)
      if not ret:
          ret = request.form.get(param)
      return ret

Note also that "if not ret:" is truthy-falsy, meaning an empty string "" from
request.args would also fall through to checking request.form, and a value of "0"
would similarly be treated as missing. This is a logic error in the guard condition.
findings.impact:
If a caller does not check for None, downstream operations like string concatenation,
SQL formatting, or file path construction will raise TypeError or produce unexpected
"None" string interpolation (e.g., f-string with None becomes the literal string "None").
The falsy check also means legitimate values like "0" or empty string are incorrectly
discarded from query parameters in favor of form data.
findings.remediation:
Use "is None" instead of "not ret" for the guard check, and provide a default return
value (e.g., empty string) or raise a ValueError when the parameter is required:

  def get_parameter(request, param):
      ret = request.args.get(param)
      if ret is None:
          ret = request.form.get(param, "")
      return ret

findings.title: Bare except clauses suppress all exceptions across 26 test case endpoints
findings.description:
Twenty-six test case files use bare "except:" clauses that catch all exceptions
including SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt, and MemoryError. This is a systemic pattern
across the codebase. Representative files include: BenchmarkTest00018.py (line 59),
BenchmarkTest00074.py (line 52), BenchmarkTest00078.py (line 54),
BenchmarkTest00265.py (line 60), BenchmarkTest00069.py (line 60), and 21 others.

SOURCE: Various dangerous operations (exec(), pickle.loads(), XPath queries, LDAP searches)
GUARD: Bare except: clause catches everything indiscriminately
SINK: Error is silently consumed; generic error message returned to user

For example in BenchmarkTest00018.py:
  try:
      run_query = lxml.etree.XPath(query)
      ...
  except:
      RESPONSE += f'Error parsing XPath Query...'

This pattern masks the true nature of failures and can hide security-relevant errors
such as out-of-memory conditions or broken parsing states.
findings.impact:
Bare except clauses can mask critical system errors (SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt,
MemoryError), making debugging impossible and potentially leaving the application in
an inconsistent state. They also prevent proper error propagation and logging, which
hinders incident response and security monitoring.
findings.remediation:
Replace bare "except:" with specific exception types. For example:
- XPath operations: except (lxml.etree.XPathError, lxml.etree.XPathSyntaxError)
- pickle.loads: except (pickle.UnpicklingError, ValueError, TypeError)
- exec(): except (SyntaxError, NameError, TypeError)
- LDAP: except ldap3.core.exceptions.LDAPException
At minimum, use "except Exception:" to avoid catching SystemExit and KeyboardInterrupt.

findings.title: Weak Random Number Generator Used for Session Tokens
findings.description:
Two test cases use Python's random module (not cryptographically secure) to generate
values used for rememberMe session cookies:
1. BenchmarkTest00025.py (line 50): value = str(random.normalvariate())[2:]
2. BenchmarkTest00026.py (line 51): value = str(random.normalvariate())[2:]
In contrast, BenchmarkTest00032.py and BenchmarkTest00033.py correctly use secrets module.
findings.impact:
Python's random module uses a Mersenne Twister PRNG that is predictable. An attacker
who observes several outputs can predict future values and forge valid session tokens.
findings.remediation:
Use the secrets module for security-sensitive random values:
import secrets; value = secrets.token_hex(32)

findings.title: Cookies Set Without Secure Flag (secure=False)
findings.description:
Three test cases set cookies with the secure flag explicitly set to False:
1. BenchmarkTest00066.py (line 65): secure=False
2. BenchmarkTest00065.py (line 65): secure=False
3. BenchmarkTest00064.py (line 64): secure=False
All three create a cookie named SomeCookie with user-derived values.
findings.impact:
Cookies without the secure flag can be intercepted by network attackers via man-in-the-middle
attacks on HTTP connections. This enables session hijacking or data theft.
findings.remediation:
Always set secure=True for cookies containing sensitive data. Enforce HTTPS across the
entire application.

findings.title: Use of Weak Cryptographic Hash Algorithm MD5 for Password Hashing
findings.description:
BenchmarkTest00057.py uses hashlib.md5() to hash sensitive data before writing to
passwordFile.txt. MD5 is cryptographically broken.
SOURCE: Cookie value (sensitive data) flows through list manipulation
GUARD: list operations (no sanitization, but bar ends up as lst[1] = 'moresafe')
SINK: hashlib.md5().update(input) -> written to passwordFile.txt

Note: Due to list manipulation, bar = lst[1] = 'moresafe' (a constant), making this
a false positive for data flow. However, using MD5 for any password hashing is a
cryptographic weakness. BenchmarkTest00058.py uses SHA-1 with a similar safe data flow.
findings.impact:
MD5 is cryptographically broken and vulnerable to collision attacks. SHA-1 is deprecated.
Both can be brute-forced using rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated attacks.
findings.remediation:
Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 for password hashing. For general-purpose hashing,
use SHA-256 or SHA-3.

findings.title: Unreachable code after return in GET handlers across all cookie-setting test cases
findings.description:
Approximately 50 test case GET handlers contain unreachable code after a return
statement. The pattern is:

  def BenchmarkTestXXXXX_get():
      response = make_response(...)
      response.set_cookie(...)
      return response
      return BenchmarkTestXXXXX_post()  # <-- unreachable

SOURCE: Code after return statement
GUARD: N/A - Python's return semantics make the second return unreachable
SINK: The _post() function is never called from the GET handler

This appears in BenchmarkTest00001.py (line 32), BenchmarkTest00002.py (line 32),
BenchmarkTest00012.py (line 32), BenchmarkTest00018.py (line 32),
BenchmarkTest00069.py (line 32), BenchmarkTest00074.py (line 32),
BenchmarkTest00077.py (line 32), BenchmarkTest00078.py (line 32), and many others.

While not a security vulnerability itself, unreachable code suggests either a
copy-paste error or an incomplete refactor. If the intent was to chain GET to POST,
the logic is broken.
findings.impact:
The unreachable code has no runtime effect but indicates potential logic errors
in the test case design. If the intended behavior was for GET requests to also
execute POST logic, that behavior is silently not happening.
findings.remediation:
Remove the unreachable return statement, or if the intent is to chain GET to POST,
restructure the code so the POST handler is called before the first return:

  # If chaining is intended:
  def BenchmarkTestXXXXX_get():
      response = make_response(...)
      response.set_cookie(...)
      return response  # Remove the dead code below

findings.title: db_sqlite.results() outputs database content without HTML escaping
findings.description:
The results() function in db_sqlite.py outputs the SQL query string directly
into HTML response content on line 43:
`ret += f'Results set is empty for query: {sql}'`

It also outputs database row values on line 47 without HTML escaping:
`ret += ', '.join(str(x) for x in row)`

If any database column contains user-controlled data (which it can in the
benchmark scenario where user input is inserted via SQL injection), the unescaped
output creates a reflected XSS vector through the database.

The escape_for_html() function exists in utils.py and is used elsewhere, but
db_sqlite.py does not import or use it.
findings.impact:
Database content rendered without escaping can lead to stored XSS if
user-controlled data reaches the database and is then displayed back. In this
benchmark context, several SQL injection test cases insert user data that flows
through this results function.
findings.remediation:
Import and apply escape_for_html() to both the SQL string and the row data
in the results() function output.

findings.title: Global mutable state used for session management instead of Flask sessions
findings.description:
The mysession dictionary in helpers/utils.py (line 6) is a module-level global
mutable dictionary used as a makeshift session store by test cases (e.g.,
BenchmarkTest00025). This is used alongside Flask's actual session mechanism
(via flask.session in trust boundary test cases), creating two parallel session
mechanisms.

The global mysession dict is shared across all requests and all users, has no
expiration, no isolation between users, and is not thread-safe. Flask's built-in
session support (already configured via app.secret_key in app.py) would be the
correct mechanism.
findings.impact:
The global dictionary creates race conditions in concurrent request handling.
Data from one user's session can leak to another user. The dual session mechanism
(mysession + flask.session) makes it unclear which is authoritative and increases
cognitive load for reviewers.
findings.remediation:
Replace mysession usage with Flask's flask.session or flask.g for
request-scoped data. Remove the global mysession dictionary from utils.py.

findings.title: Dead code: unreachable return RESPONSE after flask.redirect() in redirect test cases
findings.description:
In BenchmarkTest00067.py (and similarly in BenchmarkTest00068.py), there is
a `return RESPONSE` statement after `return flask.redirect(bar)`. The return RESPONSE
on line 49 can never be reached because flask.redirect(bar) on line 47 always returns
first.

This is distinct from the dead code in GET handlers -- this is dead code within the
POST handler logic itself, creating an impossible code path that makes it appear the
function might return a regular response when it always redirects.
findings.impact:
Unreachable code after return statements confuses control flow analysis and may
cause security scanners to produce false findings about the RESPONSE variable.
findings.remediation:
Remove the unreachable `return RESPONSE` statements from the affected redirect
test POST handlers.

findings.title: Inconsistent Flask import pattern: dual import of flask module in 10 test files
findings.description:
Ten test files (BenchmarkTest00067, 00068, 00069, 00070, 00071, 00072, 00151,
00153, 00258, 00260) do `import flask` inside the POST handler body, after already
having `from flask import redirect, url_for, request, make_response, render_template`
at the module level. Five of these use flask.redirect() (ignoring the imported
redirect), and five use flask.session (which was not imported at the module level).

This creates two inconsistent patterns:
- Module-level named imports: from flask import redirect, request, ...
- Function-level full module import: import flask then flask.redirect() or
  flask.session['key']
findings.impact:
Inconsistent import patterns across test files make it harder to search for
framework usage patterns, perform code audits, and maintain consistent coding
standards. Code reviewers and static analysis tools must account for both patterns.
findings.remediation:
Standardize on one approach. Preferred: add session to the module-level
from flask import line for files that need it, and use the already-imported
redirect instead of flask.redirect. Remove the function-level import flask.

findings.title: Module-level database initialization executes on import with no error handling
findings.description:
The db_sqlite.py module executes database schema creation (DROP TABLE, CREATE TABLE,
INSERT) at module import time (lines 6-28). This code runs as a side effect of
`import helpers.db_sqlite` with no error handling, no connection pooling, and no
protection against concurrent access.

The module-level connection on line 6 is opened, used for schema setup, and closed
on line 28. The get_connection() function on line 30 creates new connections. This
means every time the application starts or the module is re-imported, the database
is dropped and recreated, destroying any data.
findings.impact:
1. Any test data accumulated during a session is lost on restart.
2. The module-level execution makes it impossible to import db_sqlite for testing
   or inspection without triggering the schema reset side effect.
3. No error handling means a filesystem permission issue or missing directory
   will crash the entire application on startup with an unhandled exception.
4. The sqlite3 connections returned by get_connection() are not thread-safe by
   default, which is problematic in a Flask application serving concurrent requests.
findings.remediation:
1. Move schema initialization into an explicit init_db() function called from
   app.py startup, rather than running it as a module-level side effect.
2. Add error handling around the database initialization.
3. Consider using Flask application context and g object for connection management.

findings.title: SQLAlchemy and Tornado listed as dependencies but never used
findings.description:
The requirements.txt lists SQLAlchemy 2.0.40 (line 87) and Tornado 6.4.2 (line 91)
as dependencies, but neither is imported or used anywhere in the codebase.

Database access is performed exclusively via the sqlite3 standard library module
in helpers/db_sqlite.py. The web framework is Flask, not Tornado. There are no
references to sqlalchemy, SQLAlchemy, tornado, or any Tornado imports in any
Python file in the project.

Additionally, many other dependencies appear to be scanning/testing tool dependencies
rather than application dependencies (bandit, mitmproxy, wapiti, etc.) that should
be separated into dev/test requirements.
findings.impact:
Unnecessary dependencies increase the attack surface, installation time, and
potential for supply chain vulnerabilities. SQLAlchemy and Tornado are substantial
packages with their own dependency trees. Including unused dependencies violates
the principle of least privilege for package management.
findings.remediation:
1. Remove SQLAlchemy and Tornado from requirements.txt since they are unused.
2. Separate scanning/testing tools (bandit, mitmproxy, wapiti-related packages)
   into a separate requirements-dev.txt file.
3. Audit remaining dependencies against actual imports to remove other unused packages.

findings.title: Unused import: escape_for_html imported but unused in 30 of 70 test files
findings.description:
30 out of 70 test files import `escape_for_html` from `helpers.utils` at line 19
but never reference it in the file body. The affected files include all SQL injection,
weak randomness, secure cookie, redirect, trust boundary, and LDAP injection test
cases that do not produce HTML output requiring escaping.

Affected files: BenchmarkTest00011, 00012, 00025, 00026, 00027, 00032, 00033,
00067, 00068, 00069, 00078-00080, 00082, 00083, 00096-00099, 00151, 00164-00167,
00258, 00269, 00283, 00284, 00350, 00512.
findings.impact:
Unused imports add noise to code review and static analysis. They create false
dependency relationships that obscure the actual module coupling graph.
findings.remediation:
Remove the `from helpers.utils import escape_for_html` line from the 30 test files
that do not reference `escape_for_html` in their handler code.

findings.title: Unused import: redirect imported but flask.redirect used instead in redirect test cases
findings.description:
Five test files (BenchmarkTest00067, 00068, 00069, 00151, 00258) import `redirect`
from flask at the module level via `from flask import redirect, ...` but then
separately do `import flask` inside the POST handler and call `flask.redirect(bar)`
instead of using the already-imported `redirect`. This means `redirect` is imported
but unused in these files, and the pattern is inconsistent with the module-level
import style.

Meanwhile, in the remaining 65 test files, `redirect` is imported but never called
at all (neither as `redirect()` nor as `flask.redirect()`), making it a dead import
in those files too.
findings.impact:
Inconsistent import patterns make code harder to maintain and review. The dual
import of flask (module-level named import + function-level `import flask`) is
confusing and suggests the redirect test cases were written with a different
pattern than the rest of the codebase.
findings.remediation:
For the 5 redirect test files: remove `import flask` from inside the handler and
use the already-imported `redirect(bar)` instead of `flask.redirect(bar)`.
For all other files: remove `redirect` from the import line since it is unused.

findings.title: Unused import: url_for imported in all 70 test files but never used
findings.description:
All 70 test case files contain the identical import line:
`from flask import redirect, url_for, request, make_response, render_template`

The `url_for` function is never used in any test file. It is imported in all 70
files but there are zero references to it beyond the import statements.
findings.impact:
Unused imports add unnecessary coupling to Flask internals, increase module load
time (marginally), and generate noise for static analysis and linting tools.
They obscure the actual dependencies of each module.
findings.remediation:
Remove `url_for` from the import line in all 70 test files. The import should be:
`from flask import redirect, request, make_response, render_template`
or further trimmed per file based on actual usage.

findings.title: Dead code: unreachable return statement after return in all GET handlers
findings.description:
Every GET handler in all 70 test case files contains an unreachable return statement
after the initial `return response`. For example in BenchmarkTest00001.py line 32:
`return BenchmarkTest00001_post()` appears after `return response` on line 31.
This pattern is replicated across all 70 test files. The dead code suggests a
copy-paste artifact where the GET handler was intended to optionally delegate to
the POST handler but the early return was added without removing the fallthrough.
findings.impact:
Dead code increases maintenance burden and confuses code analysis tools. Security
scanners may misinterpret the control flow, leading to false negatives if they
follow the unreachable path or false positives from dead code analysis.
findings.remediation:
Remove the unreachable `return BenchmarkTestNNNNN_post()` line from all 70 GET
handlers. This line can never execute due to the preceding `return response`.
